task.title:
> Build `orchestrator/redline.py`. Given a `CatalystContract` recomputed after a change,
> and the previously approved version of the same contract, produce a `ChallengeCard`
> using the ported `build_challenge`.
>
> Critical constraint: Granite receives the contract's rationale text and a description
> of what moved **in directions, not values** ("the funding gap falls sharply", never
> "the gap fell 9.2 months"). The application renders every figure. Read
> `as_directions()` in `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk/engine/scenario.py` for the
> pattern, then implement the equivalent here.
>
> Write a test that feeds Granite a breach and asserts no figure appears in its output
> that was not in its input.

**Accept when:** the fabrication test passes against live Granite, and a scripted
amendment produces a challenge card with a classification and a drafted memo.
Also: the system prompt does not say what the metrics mean. A live call just
now read "negative gap_months" as the trial running late, when it means the
money runs out before the readout. Define the metrics in the prompt: gap_months
is signed months of runway surplus at the registered completion date, negative
meaning cash is exhausted first; max_days_expired is how long the registry
showed a date that had already passed.

> Build `orchestrator/redline.py`. Given a `CatalystContract` recomputed after a change,
> and the previously approved version of the same contract, produce a `ChallengeCard`
> using the ported `build_challenge`.
>
> Critical constraint: Granite receives the contract's rationale text and a description
> of what moved **in directions, not values** ("the funding gap falls sharply", never
> "the gap fell 9.2 months"). The application renders every figure. Read
> `as_directions()` in `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk/engine/scenario.py` for the
> pattern, then implement the equivalent here.
>
> Write a test that feeds Granite a breach and asserts no figure appears in its output
> that was not in its input.

**Accept when:** the fabrication test passes against live Granite, and a scripted
amendment produces a challenge card with a classification and a drafted memo.
Also: the system prompt does not say what the metrics mean. A live call just
now read "negative gap_months" as the trial running late, when it means the
money runs out before the readout. Define the metrics in the prompt: gap_months
is signed months of runway surplus at the registered completion date, negative
meaning cash is exhausted first; max_days_expired is how long the registry
showed a date that had already passed.

data.content:
<role_definition>
You are building an auditor for biotech catalyst theses. A company claims it has cash to reach a trial readout. The cash side comes from SEC filings, the date side from a registry the sponsor controls and can revise at will, and nothing reconciles a revision against the thesis that depended on the old date. This project is that reconciliation.
The whole claim rests on every figure being traceable to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry version, so you are precise about provenance and you would rather ask than guess at an interface you have not read. You match the house style you find rather than importing habits from elsewhere.

</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.

<project_rules>

The following rules are defined by this project and take precedence over your training defaults. They apply to every response for the duration of this session — do not revert to your defaults as the conversation grows longer. Before taking any action, check whether a project rule applies. Where rules from different sources conflict, the more specific source takes precedence: workspace rules override global rules, and mode-specific rules override common rules.

# Project Instructions (AGENTS.md)

# AGENTS.md — for IBM Bob

`CLAUDE.md` sits beside this file and is addressed to a different tool. Where the
two disagree about who builds what, this file governs your work.

**You are the primary development tool on this project.** The build prompts in
`docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` are addressed to you. Claude verifies your output, runs the
kill gates, and writes docs; it does not execute those prompts.

Read `HANDOFF.md` first, then `docs/SPEC.md`. Read `docs/FINDINGS.md` before
touching any extraction code: it records eight failure modes found empirically,
and every one of them produces a wrong number rather than an error.

## What this is

Every biotech thesis rests on a date. The company has cash into X, the trial
reads out before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side
comes from SEC filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the
sponsor sets it, can revise it whenever it likes, and nothing reconciles that
revision against the thesis that depended on the old date.

This project treats that as a contract and audits it.

## The rule everything follows

**Python computes. Granite judges prose. Humans decide.**

No model-produced number reaches the user. `_fabricated()` in the ported
`granite.py` enforces it and is ported byte for byte:

- Do **not** tighten it to ban all digits. That rejects correct output quoting a
  figure from the belief's own claim text, which is quotation, not invention.
- Do **not** loosen it. The rule is "a number absent from the input".

## Do not rewrite the engine

`engine/runway.py`, `engine/ctgov_history.py` and `engine/gap.py` predate you and
are verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. They are the reason
this project is credible. Run all three before and after any change:

```bash
python3 -m engine.runway
python3 -m engine.ctgov_history
python3 -m engine.gap
```

If a change of yours needs one of them modified, stop and say so rather than
editing it. That has been the right call every time it has come up.

## Four invariants

**Every displayed number traces to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry
version.** `Runway.provenance` exists for this. A figure that cannot name its
source does not get displayed.

**Unreliable rows are shown, never ranked, never silently dropped.** A screen
that hides its hard cases is worse than one that shows them. See
`Runway.reliable`. Arrowhead computes to a 1,116 month runway in this engine
because of a partnership upfront; the answer is to flag it, not to hide it.

**A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst.** You cannot run out of money
before an event that already happened. The binding catalyst is the nearest
registered primary completion still in the future. Lapsed dates are retained and
surfaced as date-integrity signals, which is the entire point of the project.

**Burn is a band, not a point.** One quarter containing a partnership upfront is
unrepresentative, and a single number would hide that.

## Language

Say **registered primary-completion expectation**, never "readout date". They
differ systematically by two to four months and the gap is always optimistic.

Say "we could not find", never "nobody has". A commercial screener and a
published revision dataset both exist and are cited in `docs/DEMO.md`. The claim
is the monitor, not a novel dataset and not a finding.

## Voice and commits

Comments say why, not what. No em dashes. Plain language, no filler. Commit as
kristenharim with plain messages, and never add AI attribution, `Co-Authored-By`,
or generated-with footers to any commit or file.

## Never touch

`~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` is a separate working project and the fallback
demo for this submission. Phase 2 copies four files out of it, listed in
`docs/PORT.md`. Read those. Change nothing there, ever.

## Log what you build

Add a row to `docs/BOB_LOG.md` for each task, naming the tool. The README's Bob
section is written from that file rather than from memory, so a line that says
"by hand" must never be described later as yours.

</project_rules>

> Build `orchestrator/redline.py`. Given a `CatalystContract` recomputed after a change,
> and the previously approved version of the same contract, produce a `ChallengeCard`
> using the ported `build_challenge`.
>
> Critical constraint: Granite receives the contract's rationale text and a description
> of what moved **in directions, not values** ("the funding gap falls sharply", never
> "the gap fell 9.2 months"). The application renders every figure. Read
> `as_directions()` in `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk/engine/scenario.py` for the
> pattern, then implement the equivalent here.
>
> Write a test that feeds Granite a breach and asserts no figure appears in its output
> that was not in its input.

**Accept when:** the fabrication test passes against live Granite, and a scripted
amendment produces a challenge card with a classification and a drafted memo.
Also: the system prompt does not say what the metrics mean. A live call just
now read "negative gap_months" as the trial running late, when it means the
money runs out before the readout. Define the metrics in the prompt: gap_months
is signed months of runway surplus at the registered completion date, negative
meaning cash is exhausted first; max_days_expired is how long the registry
showed a date that had already passed.
</user_query>

1 | # Handoff: start here
2 | 
3 | This repo is a de-risked starting point, not a finished project. Three engine modules
4 | are built and verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. Everything else is
5 | specified but unbuilt, and that is deliberate: the unbuilt parts are what IBM Bob should
6 | build, because the challenge requires Bob to be the primary development tool.
7 | 
8 | ## What to do with the IBM chat you already have open
9 | 
10 | Pause it. Do not redirect it mid thread.
11 | 
12 | That thread's context is saturated with the portfolio risk framing, so a correction
13 | inside it will keep drifting back to belief cards about VaR. A pivot this size wants a
14 | clean context whose first input is a written spec.
15 | 
16 | Also: do not touch `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. It runs, it talks to live Granite,
17 | and it is the fallback demo if this build stalls. Its value is that it exists.
18 | 
19 | **Open a new chat and paste this as the first message:**
20 | 
21 | > I am building the Catalyst Integrity Desk for the IBM AI Builders Challenge, with IBM
22 | > Bob as the primary development tool. The repo is at `~/projects/catalyst-integrity-desk`.
23 | >
24 | > Read these first, in order: `HANDOFF.md`, `docs/SPEC.md`, `docs/FINDINGS.md`,
25 | > `docs/PORT.md`. `docs/FINDINGS.md` contains eight data gotchas that were found
26 | > empirically and each one will cost you a day if you rediscover it.
27 | >
28 | > The three modules in `engine/` are verified working. Do not rewrite them. Run
29 | > `python3 -m engine.runway`, `python3 -m engine.ctgov_history`, and `python3 -m engine.gap`
30 | > to confirm before you change anything.
31 | >
32 | > Start with Phase 1 in `docs/SPEC.md`. Use `docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` for the sequenced
33 | > build prompts.
34 | 
35 | ## What is verified working, right now
36 | 
37 | Run these. They hit live APIs and assert on real data.
38 | 
39 | ```
40 | python3 -m engine.runway          # cash runway from SEC XBRL, 12 clinical-stage names
41 | python3 -m engine.ctgov_history   # registry revision history for one trial
42 | python3 -m engine.gap             # the join: funding gap + date integrity
43 | ```
44 | 
45 | Measured on 2026-07-21:
46 | 
47 | - `NCT04613596` (Mirati): 95 protocol versions, 6 of which moved the primary
48 |   completion date, net slip +2,193 days.
49 | - `NCT04248439` (Rocket Pharmaceuticals): **carried a completion date that had already
50 |   passed for 677 days** before correcting it.
51 | - 12 of 12 clinical-stage, pre-revenue names produced rankable runway bands. Sana
52 |   8.1-9.2 months, Prime Medicine 9.5-10.4, Rocket 9.5-9.6.
53 | 
54 | ## The one paragraph version of the idea
55 | 
56 | Every biotech thesis rests on a date: the company has cash into X, the trial reads out
57 | before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side comes from SEC
58 | filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the sponsor sets it, can
59 | revise it at any time, and the revision is not reconciled against the thesis that
60 | depended on the old date.
61 | 
62 | The Catalyst Integrity Desk treats that as a contract: this company has capital to reach
63 | this readout by this date. Python computes both sides from filed tags and registry
64 | records, with no model near a number. Granite reads the analyst's written rationale and
65 | reports which stated assumption a new filing or amendment breaks. The analyst approves,
66 | and the decision hash chains into a tamper evident ledger.
67 | 
68 | So the desk carries a third column: how the sponsor has behaved toward its own date.
69 | 
70 | ## How to frame it, exactly
71 | 
72 | **"An auditable monitor for when a portfolio's cash-to-catalyst assumption breaks."**
73 | 
74 | Not "a novel dataset" and not "a new finding". Both of those lose under one question:
75 | 
76 | - The catalyst screen is a shipping commercial product. BiopharmaWatch filters 11,000+
77 |   readouts by cash runway and burn across 949 companies, including a trial-change field.
78 | - The revision panel is a published dataset: 4.3M per-version rows across ~583k trials.
79 | - The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent published work (Guenzel & Liu, RFS 2026).
80 | 
81 | See `docs/FINDINGS.md` section 2. What survives is the *monitor*: a contract that is
82 | recomputed deterministically, breached visibly, judged against its own written rationale,
83 | and changed only by a human whose decision is hash chained. That is a real thing to build
84 | and an honest thing to claim.
85 | 
86 | ## What must not be claimed
87 | 
88 | That cash constrained sponsors revise their readout dates differently than solvent ones.
89 | **Hypothesis, untested, and adjacent work already exists.** Say "we could not find" and
90 | never "nobody has".
91 | 
92 | State the identification problem out loud: low runway correlates with small, under
93 | resourced companies that slip more anyway, which pushes the opposite way from the
94 | strategic story. A judge who hears an honest "here is the panel, here is why the causal
95 | claim is hard" scores it above a confident result that dies to one question.
96 | 
97 | Also: say **registered primary-completion expectation**, not readout date. They are not
98 | the same thing and the difference is a systematic 2 to 4 month optimistic bias.
99 |

Contents of file docs/SPEC.md:

1 | # Catalyst Integrity Desk: build spec
2 | 
3 | Ten days. Four phases. Phase 1 is done. Phases 2 to 4 are what Bob builds.
4 | 
5 | Division of labor, which is the credibility backbone and must hold everywhere:
6 | **Python computes. Granite judges prose. Humans decide.** No model produced number ever
7 | reaches the user, and that is enforced by scanning model output for figures absent from
8 | its input, not merely asserted in a README.
9 | 
10 | ## Phase 1: deterministic engine (DONE, do not rewrite)
11 | 
12 | `engine/runway.py` cash runway from SEC XBRL, as a band, with provenance and
13 | reliability flags.
14 | `engine/ctgov_history.py` registry revision history per trial, with notice and expiry
15 | metrics.
16 | `engine/gap.py` the join: funding gap plus date integrity, as a `CatalystContract`.
17 | 
18 | Each has a `demo()` that asserts against live data. Run all three before changing
19 | anything.
20 | 
21 | ## Phase 2: governance layer, ported (2 days)
22 | 
23 | Port four modules from `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. They are domain agnostic. See
24 | `docs/PORT.md` for exactly what changes. Do not copy `metrics.py` or `scenario.py`.
25 | 
26 | The unit of belief becomes the catalyst contract:
27 | 
28 | ```yaml
29 | id: RCKT-FA-2026
30 | claim: >
31 |   Rocket reaches the Fanconi anemia primary completion on cash in hand, without
32 |   a dilutive raise, and the readout resolves the gene therapy platform thesis.
33 | metric: gap_months
34 | range: [0, null]
35 | driver: "9.5 months runway against a 2026-05 registered completion"
36 | conviction: medium
37 | invalidation:
38 |   - registered completion date moves beyond runway exhaustion
39 |   - burn band widens past 3x
40 |   - securities tag path changes between quarters
41 | ```
42 | 
43 | A breach fires when a recomputed contract leaves its approved range. `scan_breaches`
44 | already takes a flat `{metric_id: value}` dict, so a contract packet plugs in unchanged.
45 | 
46 | ## Phase 3: the monitoring loop and the console (3 days)
47 | 
48 | **Redline.** A registry amendment or a new 10-Q lands. Python recomputes the contract.
49 | If the gap crosses zero, build a challenge card: which approved assumption broke, with
50 | the before and after figures rendered by the application, never by the model.
51 | 
52 | **Granite's job.** Given the contract's own rationale text and a description of the
53 | breach *in directions rather than values* ("gap falls sharply", not "gap fell 9.2
54 | months"), classify into the four labels and draft the memo. Handing it values means it
55 | quotes them and then does arithmetic on them, which is the fabrication path the risk
56 | desk already hit and fixed. Give it nothing to echo.
57 | 
58 | **Human verdict.** Accept, edit, or reject. Accepts append to the hash chained ledger,
59 | rejects go to a separate review log with the reason.
60 | 
61 | **Console.** A web view, because a terminal recording is not a product. Three screens:
62 | the contract list ranked by gap, one contract detail with its revision timeline, and the
63 | pending redline awaiting approval. This is the highest visual payoff work in the project
64 | and it carries zero risk to the engine.
65 | 
66 | ## Phase 4: the panel and the demo (3 days)
67 | 
68 | **Assemble the panel.** For 60 to 100 clinical-stage companies (not the universe, and
69 | say so out loud rather than implying full coverage): quarterly runway from DERA, joined
70 | to full revision histories for their live Phase 2 and 3 trials.
71 | 
72 | Ship the descriptive panel. The causal claim is stated as the open question with its
73 | identification problem named. See `HANDOFF.md`, "What must not be claimed".
74 | 
75 | **Freeze a snapshot.** The demo reads from a local JSON snapshot, never a live API. Live
76 | registry calls on stage are the most common demo failure there is.
77 | 
78 | **Record a backup video.** Non negotiable.
79 | 
80 | ## Scope discipline
81 | 
82 | In scope: one ranked contract list, one contract detail with revision timeline, one
83 | scripted redline event, human approve and reject, the descriptive panel, hash chained
84 | ledger with visible tamper detection.
85 | 
86 | Out of scope, and say so if asked rather than pretending: full universe coverage,
87 | valuation, competitor analysis, probability of success modeling, multi user, the causal
88 | result.
89 | 
90 | ## 48 hour kill gates
91 | 
92 | Run these before committing the remaining eight days. If a gate fails, fall back to the
93 | Deliberate Risk Desk rather than spending the week discovering the same thing slower.
94 | 
95 | | Gate | Pass condition | On failure |
96 | |---|---|---|
97 | | History | 10 candidate trials yield at least 3 material primary-completion-date revisions | fall back to A |
98 | | Join | at least 5 clean issuer to sponsor to SEC filing joins | fall back to A |
99 | | Finance | runway reconciles to the filing by hand, with provenance, for one company | manual single-company demo, or A |
100 | | Demo | one breach explains itself in 20 seconds, out loud, to someone else | simplify before adding anything |
101 | 
102 | The demo gate is the one people skip and the one that decides the outcome. A judge has
103 | two or three minutes. If the breach does not land in twenty seconds, no amount of
104 | governance architecture behind it will help.
105 | 
106 | ## Definition of done
107 | 
108 | - [ ] `python3 -m engine.gap` still passes after every change
109 | - [ ] Ledger `verify()` returns false on a one byte edit, on camera
110 | - [ ] Granite emits no figure absent from its input, with a test that proves it
111 | - [ ] Every displayed number traces to a named XBRL tag or a registry version
112 | - [ ] Flagged rows are visible and unranked, never silently dropped
113 | - [ ] Demo runs from a frozen snapshot with the network off
114 | - [ ] README describes what Bob built versus what preceded it, accurately
115 |

Contents of file docs/PORT.md:

1 | # Porting the governance layer
2 | 
3 | Four modules move from `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. They were written for a
4 | portfolio risk desk and are domain agnostic anyway, which is why this pivot is a port
5 | and not a rewrite. Line counts verified 2026-07-21.
6 | 
7 | **Copy the source repo, do not move it.** `deliberate-risk-desk` runs, talks to live
8 | Granite, and is the fallback demo.
9 | 
10 | | File | Lines | Domain coupling | Change needed |
11 | |---|---|---|---|
12 | | `engine/ledger.py` | 174 | one docstring example, the `scope` field comment | comments only |
13 | | `orchestrator/challenge.py` | 135 | none | none |
14 | | `orchestrator/classifier.py` | 68 | none | none |
15 | | `orchestrator/granite.py` | 318 | `SYSTEM_PROMPT` and `ACTION_PROMPT` text | rewrite two prompt strings |
16 | 
17 | 695 lines, of which the only real work is two prompts.
18 | 
19 | ## Why it drops in cleanly
20 | 
21 | `scan_breaches(cards, packet_flat)` takes a flat `{metric_id: value}` dict and knows
22 | nothing about what produced it. A catalyst contract packet looks like:
23 | 
24 | ```python
25 | {
26 |   "gap_months": -5.2,
27 |   "runway_months_low": 9.5,
28 |   "burn_ttm_annual": 180_000_000,
29 |   "pcd_revisions": 4,
30 |   "max_days_expired": 677,
31 | }
32 | ```
33 | 
34 | That satisfies the existing interface with no changes to the ledger.
35 | 
36 | `BeliefCard` already carries claim, metric, range, driver, and conviction, which is
37 | exactly the catalyst contract's shape. `scope` becomes `"company:RCKT"` or
38 | `"trial:NCT04248439"` instead of `"position:NVDA"`.
39 | 
40 | `Classification`'s four labels (`direct_contradiction`, `assumption_weakened`,
41 | `assumption_strengthened`, `new_material_evidence`) carry over unchanged. A trial
42 | amendment that pushes completion past runway exhaustion is a direct contradiction of a
43 | "funded to catalyst" claim, in exactly the sense the labels already mean.
44 | 
45 | ## The two prompts that need rewriting
46 | 
47 | `SYSTEM_PROMPT` currently opens "You are the challenge partner on a portfolio risk
48 | desk." The replacement frames the same job in the new domain: a deterministic engine has
49 | recomputed a catalyst contract and something left its approved range; judge whether the
50 | drift breaks the written rationale.
51 | 
52 | **Keep the fabrication guard exactly as it is.** `_fabricated()` scans model output for
53 | numeric runs absent from its input and discards the whole response on a hit. It already
54 | caught Granite quoting supplied impact figures and then subtracting them to state a loss
55 | it was never given. The structural fix was to hand the model directions instead of
56 | values, and that pattern must survive the port. Do not loosen it to "ban all digits"
57 | either: an earlier version did, and it rejected Granite for correctly quoting a figure
58 | out of the belief's own claim text. The rule is "a number absent from the input", which
59 | is what fabrication actually means.
60 | 
61 | ## What does not come across
62 | 
63 | `engine/metrics.py` and `engine/scenario.py` are finance specific and have no analogue
64 | here. Leave them.
65 | 
66 | There is also a substantive reason not to reuse `scenario.py`'s approach. Its shock
67 | scales each ticker's whole price history by a constant, which leaves returns and the
68 | covariance matrix unchanged, so every metric that moves does so only because position
69 | weights moved. That makes its headline result an artifact of the shock convention rather
70 | than a measurement. Nothing in this project should inherit that shape: here both sides
71 | of the gap come from filed values and dated registry records, and there is no assumption
72 | doing the work.
73 |

Contents of file docs/FINDINGS.md:

1 | # Findings: data gotchas, prior art, attack surface
2 | 
3 | Everything in section 1 was found by running code against the live APIs on 2026-07-21,
4 | not read in documentation. Each one silently produces a wrong number rather than an
5 | error, which is the expensive kind.
6 | 
7 | ## 1. Data gotchas, each with the fix
8 | 
9 | ### 1.1 The registry history endpoint is TLS fingerprint gated
10 | 
11 | `/api/int/studies/{nct}/history` returns 403 to `urllib` and will do the same to
12 | `requests`, because both share OpenSSL's TLS fingerprint. It returns 200 to `curl`
13 | with any User-Agent, including a bare one.
14 | 
15 | Tested four header combinations (bare UA, curl-style UA, browser UA, browser UA plus
16 | gzip and Accept) against a curl control. All four urllib variants 403, curl 200. It is
17 | not the headers.
18 | 
19 | **Fix:** shell out to curl, which ships with macOS and needs no dependency. See
20 | `engine/ctgov_history.py::_get`. `curl_cffi` would also work and adds a dependency.
21 | 
22 | ### 1.2 A cash tag can go stale while the company is fine
23 | 
24 | Arrowhead stopped filing `CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue` in 2024 and moved to
25 | `CashCashEquivalentsRestrictedCashAndRestrictedCashEquivalents`. A first-wins tag
26 | waterfall returned the last value of the abandoned tag: **$69M as of 2024-06-30, when
27 | the real figure was $3,377M as of 2026-03-31.** A 49x error, and it dated the entire
28 | record to 2024, which then broke the securities match too.
29 | 
30 | This does not look like a bug. It looks like a company about to die, which is exactly
31 | the row a distress screen puts at the top.
32 | 
33 | **Fix:** evaluate every cash tag and take the one with the most recent balance date,
34 | not the first that exists. Net out restricted cash when the winning tag bundles it.
35 | 
36 | ### 1.3 Cash flow facts are year-to-date, so "quarterly" filtering lies
37 | 
38 | Cash flow statements are cumulative within a fiscal year. Q1 is natively a 90 day fact,
39 | Q2 arrives as a 6 month figure, Q3 as 9 month, Q4 inside the 10-K as 12 month. So
40 | filtering XBRL duration facts to a 60-120 day span does not return the last four
41 | quarters. **It returns Q1 of four consecutive years**, and summing those produces a
42 | plausible looking annual burn that is the trailing twelve months of nothing.
43 | 
44 | Observed directly: Moderna's "quarterly" facts came back as 2026-03-31, 2025-03-31,
45 | 2024-03-31, 2023-03-31.
46 | 
47 | **Fix:** facts within one fiscal year share a `start`, so group on `start`, sort by
48 | `end`, and take consecutive differences. See `engine/runway.py::_quarterly_flows`.
49 | 
50 | ### 1.4 Short term investments have no single tag, and it matters most where it matters
51 | 
52 | There is no `CashCashEquivalentsAndShortTermInvestments` tag. It is never filed. Real
53 | coverage across clinical-stage filers is roughly 45%, split across at least three tags:
54 | `ShortTermInvestments`, `MarketableSecuritiesCurrent`,
55 | `AvailableForSaleSecuritiesDebtSecuritiesCurrent`. All four appear in the 12 name test
56 | set, and Ionis uses two at once.
57 | 
58 | Part of the 45% is genuine (small companies hold only cash) and part is fragmentation.
59 | Either way the missing half skews toward the well capitalized companies whose runway is
60 | most interesting.
61 | 
62 | **Fix:** sum across all securities tags, but only those struck on the same date as the
63 | cash balance, or the numerator mixes two quarters. Record which path resolved.
64 | 
65 | ### 1.5 `company_tickers.json` is missing live filers
66 | 
67 | The file is complete (10,426 entries, 870 KB) and Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD) is simply
68 | not in it. Neither is Verve.
69 | 
70 | **Fix:** the ticker map is a demo convenience only. The universe comes from the DERA
71 | Financial Statement Data Sets quarterly ZIP, keyed on CIK and SIC code, where no ticker
72 | is involved. Use SIC 2834, 2835, 2836. SIC 8731 is a red herring: 15 filings against
73 | 412 at 2834.
74 | 
75 | ### 1.6 Operating cash flow is not burn for a company with revenue
76 | 
77 | Arrowhead's trailing year contains an $825M partnership inflow, netting its burn to near
78 | zero and computing a **1,116 month runway**. Arithmetically correct, financially
79 | meaningless.
80 | 
81 | **Fix:** flag any cash-positive operating quarter in the trailing window and any case
82 | where the two burn estimates disagree by more than 3x. Flagged rows stay visible with
83 | the reason attached but never carry a rank. On the correct population (clinical-stage,
84 | pre-revenue) the flag rate went from 5 of 8 commercial names to 0 of 12.
85 | 
86 | ### 1.7 Sponsors revise dates backwards, and it is a typo
87 | 
88 | `NCT04613596` moved its completion date +1,317 days at version 92 and then -1,317 days
89 | at version 94, two months later. That is data entry, not a forecast revision. Any
90 | statistic over revision magnitudes needs a reversal filter.
91 | 
92 | ### 1.8 The sponsor to CIK join is the real engineering tax
93 | 
94 | `leadSponsor.name` is free text with no CIK. Forward matching (EDGAR name to registry)
95 | runs about 83% naively, and most misses are correct rejections (animal health, cannabis,
96 | shell companies). Sana and Intellia both failed to match in the demo. Realistic ceiling
97 | with an alias table plus hand review of the top 300 by market cap is 90-95%.
98 | 
99 | Budget a full day for this. It is the single largest unbudgeted cost in the project.
100 | 
101 | ### 1.9 `query.spons` matches collaborators, so a partner's trial becomes your catalyst
102 | 
103 | The v2 search parameter `query.spons` is not a lead-sponsor filter. It matches the
104 | sponsor/collaborator block as a whole, so a trial another company runs comes back under
105 | your issuer's name and `engine/gap.py` assigns it as that issuer's binding catalyst.
106 | 
107 | Observed: `find_trials("ARVINAS, INC.")` returns `NCT05654623` first. Its `leadSponsor`
108 | is **Pfizer**; Arvinas Estrogen Receptor, Inc. is a collaborator. Arvinas's funding gap
109 | was therefore computed against a Pfizer-run trial.
110 | 
111 | One of 8 joined names in the 12 name set. It does not error and it does not look wrong on
112 | screen, because the trial is real and the drug is genuinely theirs.
113 | 
114 | **Fix:** after searching, keep only trials whose `leadSponsor.name` matches the issuer,
115 | normalising the suffix noise (`Inc.`/`Inc`/`, Inc`/`AG`/`Corp`). The registry's lead
116 | sponsor string differs from EDGAR's registrant name in 3 of 8 joins even when correct
117 | (`Rocket Pharmaceuticals Inc.` vs `ROCKET PHARMACEUTICALS, INC.`), so match normalised,
118 | not literal.
119 | 
120 | ### 1.10 The nearest registered completion is usually the most stale one
121 | 
122 | `find_trials` sorts ascending by primary completion date and `build` takes `trials[0]`,
123 | which is the *earliest* date, not the *next* one. Because sponsors carry lapsed dates
124 | (finding 1.7 and the Rocket case), the earliest date is frequently already in the past,
125 | so the selection rule is biased toward exactly the stalest record in the set.
126 | 
127 | Three of 8 joined names picked an already-passed completion date on 2026-07-21: ARVN
128 | (2025-01-31), EDIT (2025-08), RCKT (2026-05-05).
129 | 
130 | This one is not cosmetic. Rocket is the demo's hero row. Against its picked date the gap
131 | is **+8.4 months, "funded to catalyst"**. Against its nearest *future* registered
132 | completion (`NCT06092034`, 2028-04) the same runway gives **-14.5 months, "financing
133 | required"**. Same company, same filing, opposite verdict, decided entirely by which
134 | registered date is treated as binding.
135 | 
136 | **Fix is a definition, not a patch, and it is Kristen's call:** decide whether the binding
137 | catalyst is the nearest completion date still in the future, or the nearest one whose
138 | trial is still live regardless of a lapsed date. The second is defensible and is arguably
139 | the project's whole point, but then the gap must be presented against a date the sponsor
140 | has already missed, and the demo has to say so out loud rather than print
141 | "funded to catalyst".
142 | 
143 | ## 2. Prior art, stated honestly
144 | 
145 | **The screen is not novel.** [BiopharmaWatch Catalyst Sync](https://www.biopharmawatch.com/catalyst-sync)
146 | sells exactly it: filter 11,000+ upcoming readouts by cash runway and burn rate across
147 | 949 companies. BioPharmCatalyst, Biomedtracker/Citeline, and Stifel's weekly biopharma
148 | update all occupy adjacent ground. EY Beyond Borders publishes the aggregate annually
149 | (33% of public biotechs under one year of runway at end 2025).
150 | 
151 | Do not pitch the screen. A judge finds BiopharmaWatch in one search.
152 | 
153 | **The revision panel is also not novel, and this was an overclaim in an earlier draft of
154 | this document.** [brbk/clinical_trials_history](https://huggingface.co/datasets/brbk/clinical_trials_history)
155 | is 4,333,631 rows across roughly 583,000 trials, every version, with
156 | `primary_completion_date` as a per-version field, built off the same internal endpoint.
157 | Do not say "nobody has assembled this."
158 | 
159 | Operationally this is good news: it removes the single largest engineering cost in the
160 | project. Crawling histories directly is roughly 50 to 100 requests per trial. Use the
161 | dataset for the panel and keep `engine/ctgov_history.py` for live single-trial checks in
162 | the demo, where fetching from the source is the point. Note the licence is CC-BY-NC-4.0,
163 | which covers a hackathon but not a commercial product.
164 | 
165 | **The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent, not empty.**
166 | [Guenzel & Liu, Excess Commitment in R&D, RFS 39(7) 2026](https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhag026)
167 | uses clinical trial project data and finds that delays reduce subsequent project
168 | termination, instrumented with trial site congestion, moderated by CEO stock-price
169 | sensitivity. That is the opposite arrow from this project's hypothesis (delay causing
170 | firm behaviour, versus firm finances causing date revision behaviour), but it means the
171 | territory is occupied and "no paper connects finance and trial timing" is indefensible.
172 | Cite it, distinguish the arrow, and never claim empty terrain.
173 | 
174 | **What still appears open,** stated as a search result rather than a fact: we could not
175 | find work on sponsor liquidity predicting *disclosure* behaviour toward the registered
176 | date specifically, meaning how long a lapsed date is carried and how much notice a
177 | revision gives. That is narrower than "the intersection is unoccupied" and it is the
178 | most that can be honestly claimed without a systematic review.
179 | 
180 | Background that does exist: the trial delay literature
181 | ([Shadbolt et al., JAMA Netw Open 2023](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2800488),
182 | about 1 in 5 RCTs complete on time, median delay 12.2 months), the primary outcome
183 | switching literature, and the biotech financing literature
184 | ([Lerner, Shane & Tsai, JFE 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X02002568)).
185 | 
186 | ## 3. The five hardest questions
187 | 
188 | **"Your burn number is wrong for exactly the companies that matter."**
189 | Correct. A Phase 3 ramp burns above the trailing rate; a partnership upfront shows a
190 | positive quarter. That is why burn is a band and unstable rows are excluded from
191 | ranking rather than silently included. Publish the sensitivity.
192 | 
193 | **"Primary completion date is not a readout."**
194 | Correct, and it is a systematic bias, not noise. PCD is last patient last visit for the
195 | primary endpoint; topline follows by weeks to months. The gap is optimistic by roughly
196 | 2 to 4 months on every row. The honest reframe is that the number is the sponsor's own
197 | registered expectation, which is weaker as a product and stronger as research, because
198 | the sponsor's stated belief is precisely the object of study.
199 | 
200 | **"Companies raise opportunistically, not at zero cash."**
201 | Concede fully. This is why "negative gap predicts financing" is close to tautological: a
202 | company with six months of cash raises within six months regardless. It also strengthens
203 | the real hypothesis, because if raising is window dependent then a company facing a
204 | closed window has a stronger reason to manage the visible date.
205 | 
206 | **"Survivorship."**
207 | Real. The public biotech universe shrank from 977 to 758 companies between 2021 and
208 | 2025, and the missing names are non randomly the negative gap tail. DERA is available
209 | per quarter back to 2009, so rebuild the universe as of each historical quarter. One
210 | afternoon, and it is the difference between a snapshot and a panel.
211 | 
212 | **"The interesting companies are the ones your arithmetic fails on."**
213 | Royalty monetization, non dilutive upfronts, debt facilities with milestone tranches,
214 | priority review voucher sales. Every one breaks cash divided by burn. This is the best
215 | justification for the model in the architecture: not to extract numbers, but to read the
216 | going concern and subsequent events footnotes and emit a categorical flag. Company
217 | guided runway versus computed runway is itself a chart worth showing.
218 |

Contents of file orchestrator/granite.py:

1 | """IBM Granite (watsonx.ai) plugged into the classifier seam.
2 | 
3 | Same `.classify(card, breach, context)` signature as StubClassifier, so swapping
4 | it in is a one-line change at the call site and the pipeline, ledger, and UI are
5 | untouched.
6 | 
7 | What Granite is actually for here: the stub compares numbers, which is a Python
8 | `if`. Granite's job is the semantic step the stub cannot do — read the card's
9 | claim and driver and judge whether the breach changes the *story*. A contract can
10 | sit outside its numeric band and still be fine if the thesis said so, and a small
11 | drift can be fatal if it contradicts the stated rationale. That judgment is the
12 | whole reason a model is in this loop.
13 | 
14 | Provenance rule: Granite emits NO numbers. Every figure in the final artifact is
15 | rendered by application code from the engine's own output. A rationale containing
16 | a digit is rejected as fabrication risk and the run falls back to the stub. See
17 | `_NUMBER` below.
18 | 
19 | Credentials (never hardcode):
20 |     export WATSONX_API_KEY=...
21 |     export WATSONX_PROJECT_ID=...
22 |     export WATSONX_URL=https://us-south.ml.cloud.ibm.com   # your region
23 | """
24 | from __future__ import annotations
25 | 
26 | import json
27 | import os
28 | import re
29 | import sys
30 | import time
31 | import urllib.parse
32 | import urllib.request
33 | 
34 | from engine.ledger import BeliefCard, Breach
35 | from orchestrator.classifier import (
36 |     LABELS, Classification, StubClassifier,
37 |     DIRECT_CONTRADICTION, ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED, ASSUMPTION_STRENGTHENED, NEW_MATERIAL_EVIDENCE,
38 | )
39 | 
40 | IAM_URL = "https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token"
41 | API_VERSION = "2024-10-08"
42 | # granite-3-8b-instruct still answers but watsonx returns a withdrawn-state warning for
43 | # it, and a withdrawn model can be pulled. Not something to discover while recording the
44 | # submission demo.
45 | DEFAULT_MODEL = "ibm/granite-4-h-small"
46 | 
47 | # A number is fabricated if it did not come from the input. Quoting a figure that the
48 | # card's own claim already contains ("acceptable up to ~32%") is not invention, it is
49 | # quotation, and rejecting it throws away good judgment. What must never survive is a
50 | # figure the model produced on its own.
51 | # Compared as whole numbers, not as substrings. Substring containment let real
52 | # fabrications through: a model writing "5%" passed because the input mentioned
53 | # var_95_1d_dollar, and "3%" passed because it mentioned a 30-day window. Both
54 | # were invented trade sizes presented as if measured.
55 | _NUMBER_RUN = re.compile(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?")
56 | 
57 | 
58 | def _numbers_in(text: str) -> set[str]:
59 |     return set(_NUMBER_RUN.findall(text))
60 | 
61 | # "1." at the start of a line is an option enumerator, not a claim about the book.
62 | # Stripped before the number guard runs so numbered options do not read as fabrication.
63 | _LIST_MARKER = re.compile(r"^\s*\d+[.)]\s+", re.M)
64 | 
65 | 
66 | def _fabricated(rationale: str, card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach) -> list[str]:
67 |     """Numbers in the rationale that appear nowhere in what the model was given."""
68 |     source = " ".join(str(x) for x in (
69 |         card.claim, card.driver, card.metric, card.confidence,
70 |         breach.observed, breach.expected_low, breach.expected_high,
71 |         # the model sees these rounded in the prompt, so allow both renderings
72 |         round(breach.observed), round(breach.expected_low), round(breach.expected_high),
73 |     ))
74 |     allowed = _numbers_in(source)
75 |     return [n for n in _NUMBER_RUN.findall(rationale) if n not in allowed]
76 | 
77 | SYSTEM_PROMPT = """\
78 | You are the challenge partner on a catalyst integrity desk. A deterministic engine has \
79 | already recomputed a catalyst contract and detected that something has left its approved \
80 | range. You do not compute, restate, or estimate any number.
81 | 
82 | Your job is the judgment the arithmetic cannot make: read the standing belief's claim \
83 | and its driver, then decide what the drift does to that belief's story.
84 | 
85 | Choose exactly one label:
86 | - direct_contradiction: the reading is incompatible with the claim as written.
87 | - assumption_weakened: the claim can still hold, but the reasoning behind it is eroding.
88 | - assumption_strengthened: the drift is in the direction the claim predicted.
89 | - new_material_evidence: the drift raises something the claim never accounted for.
90 | 
91 | Rules:
92 | - Reason from the claim's wording and its driver, not from how far the number moved. \
93 | A contract outside its band may still be acceptable if the claim anticipated the \
94 | condition; a small move can be serious if it undercuts the stated reason for the belief.
95 | - Weigh conviction: a low-conviction driver supporting a strong conclusion is a \
96 | weaker story than a high-conviction one.
97 | 
98 | HARD CONSTRAINT ON THE RATIONALE — this one is not stylistic, it is a correctness rule:
99 | Your rationale must contain NO digits. Not one. Do not restate the reading, do not quote \
100 | the band, do not echo a figure that appears in the claim itself. The desk's engine owns \
101 | every number and renders them beside your text; a number written by you is treated as \
102 | fabricated and your entire answer is discarded.
103 | 
104 | Say it in words instead:
105 | - NOT "gap_months is -5.2, below the approved floor of 0.0"
106 | - YES "the contract now sits below its approved floor"
107 | - NOT "conviction is 2 out of 5"
108 | - YES "the desk's conviction in this driver is weak"
109 | 
110 | Reply with JSON only, no prose and no code fences:
111 | {"label": "<one label>", "confidence": <0.0-1.0>, "rationale": "<two or three sentences, no digits>"}"""
112 | 
113 | 
114 | ACTION_PROMPT = """\
115 | You are the integrity desk's preparation partner. A scenario has been specified by a \
116 | human and priced by a deterministic engine. Your job is the last step: argue what the \
117 | desk should decide NOW, while nothing is happening and nobody is panicking.
118 | 
119 | Write three short parts, plain prose, no headings other than the ones given:
120 | 
121 | WHAT THIS ACTUALLY DOES TO THE CONTRACT
122 | One paragraph. Say plainly what the impact figures mean for the funded-to-catalyst \
123 | claim. If a metric IMPROVES while the contract becomes more strained, say so directly \
124 | and explain why it is an artifact of the measurement convention rather than a genuine \
125 | improvement. Do not let a reassuring number pass without comment.
126 | 
127 | OPTIONS
128 | Two or three concrete choices the desk could pre-commit to, each with what it costs and \
129 | what it gives up. Include the option of deliberately doing nothing where that is defensible. \
130 | These are options for a human to choose between, not a recommendation to execute.
131 | 
132 | WHAT WOULD HAVE TO BE TRUE
133 | State the trigger that should make the desk act, and the observation that would tell it \
134 | this scenario is actually starting rather than noise.
135 | 
136 | HARD CONSTRAINT ON NUMBERS — this is a correctness rule, not a style note:
137 | Your draft must contain NO digits at all. Not one.
138 | 
139 | You have deliberately been given directions rather than values, because the desk's engine \
140 | owns every figure and renders them beside your text. You do not know the numbers, so you \
141 | cannot state them, size a trade with them, or do arithmetic on them. Any digit in your \
142 | draft is therefore something you invented, and the entire draft is discarded.
143 | 
144 | Write quantities in words instead:
145 | - NOT "the gap has narrowed to -5 months"
146 | - YES "the contract now shows a negative gap — the money runs out before the readout"
147 | - NOT "burn is 180,000,000 per year"
148 | - YES "the burn rate has increased materially"
149 | 
150 | Other rules:
151 | - You cannot trade, execute, or approve anything. Everything you write is a proposal a \
152 | human must accept, edit, or reject.
153 | - No headings beyond the three above, no bullet symbols, no markdown."""
154 | 
155 | 
156 | def _user_prompt(card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach, context: dict) -> str:
157 |     # Numbers go IN (the model needs the reading to judge it) but never come back out.
158 |     parts = [
159 |         f"STANDING BELIEF ({card.card_id}, version {card.version})",
160 |         f"  scope: {card.scope}",
161 |         f"  claim: {card.claim}",
162 |         f"  driver: {card.driver}",
163 |         f"  conviction in that driver: {card.confidence} out of 5",
164 |         f"  approved band for {card.metric}: {breach.expected_low} to {breach.expected_high}",
165 |         "",
166 |         "ENGINE READING",
167 |         f"  {breach.metric} is now {breach.observed}, which is {breach.direction} the band.",
168 |     ]
169 |     if context.get("news"):
170 |         parts += ["", "EVIDENCE (verbatim, do not paraphrase as fact)"]
171 |         parts += [f"  - {snippet}" for snippet in context["news"]]
172 |     parts += ["", "Does this drift change the belief's story? Reply with the JSON object only."]
173 |     return "\n".join(parts)
174 | 
175 | 
176 | def _post(url: str, body: bytes, headers: dict, timeout: int) -> dict:
177 |     req = urllib.request.Request(url, data=body, headers=headers, method="POST")
178 |     with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=timeout) as resp:
179 |         return json.loads(resp.read())
180 | 
181 | 
182 | def _extract_json(text: str) -> dict:
183 |     """Models wrap JSON in fences or chatter often enough to be worth one line of defense."""
184 |     start, end = text.find("{"), text.rfind("}")
185 |     if start == -1 or end <= start:
186 |         raise ValueError(f"no JSON object in model output: {text[:200]!r}")
187 |     return json.loads(text[start:end + 1])
188 | 
189 | 
190 | class GraniteClassifier:
191 |     """Granite over the watsonx.ai chat API. Falls back to the stub on any failure —
192 |     a broken key must never take down the challenge loop mid-demo."""
193 | 
194 |     def __init__(self, api_key: str | None = None, project_id: str | None = None,
195 |                  url: str | None = None, model_id: str | None = None,
196 |                  transport=None, fallback=None, timeout: int = 30):
197 |         self.api_key = api_key or os.environ.get("WATSONX_API_KEY", "")
198 |         self.project_id = project_id or os.environ.get("WATSONX_PROJECT_ID", "")
199 |         self.url = (url or os.environ.get("WATSONX_URL", "https://us-south.ml.cloud.ibm.com")).rstrip("/")
200 |         self.model_id = model_id or os.environ.get("WATSONX_MODEL_ID", DEFAULT_MODEL)
201 |         self.timeout = timeout
202 |         # transport(messages) -> raw chat response dict. Injected in tests so the
203 |         # prompt and parsing are checked against recorded output, no credentials.
204 |         self._transport = transport or self._call_watsonx
205 |         self._fallback = fallback or StubClassifier()
206 |         self._token = ""
207 |         self._token_expires = 0.0
208 | 
209 |     # --- auth ---
210 |     def _bearer(self) -> str:
211 |         # ponytail: refresh a minute early rather than handling a 401 retry path.
212 |         if self._token and time.time() < self._token_expires - 60:
213 |             return self._token
214 |         if not self.api_key:
215 |             raise RuntimeError("WATSONX_API_KEY is not set")
216 |         body = urllib.parse.urlencode({
217 |             "grant_type": "urn:ibm:params:oauth:grant-type:apikey",
218 |             "apikey": self.api_key,
219 |         }).encode()
220 |         data = _post(IAM_URL, body,
221 |                      {"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"}, self.timeout)
222 |         self._token = data["access_token"]
223 |         self._token_expires = time.time() + data.get("expires_in", 3600)
224 |         return self._token
225 | 
226 |     # --- transport ---
227 |     def _call_watsonx(self, messages: list[dict]) -> dict:
228 |         if not self.project_id:
229 |             raise RuntimeError("WATSONX_PROJECT_ID is not set")
230 |         body = json.dumps({
231 |             "model_id": self.model_id,
232 |             "project_id": self.project_id,
233 |             "messages": messages,
234 |             # A cap, not a target: classification stops after a sentence or two, while
235 |             # the scenario Action needs room for three sections. Sized for the longer
236 |             # of the two so the draft never lands truncated mid-sentence.
237 |             "max_tokens": 900,
238 |             "temperature": 0,     # the same breach must classify the same way every run
239 |         }).encode()
240 |         return _post(
241 |             f"{self.url}/ml/v1/text/chat?version={API_VERSION}", body,
242 |             {"Content-Type": "application/json", "Accept": "application/json",
243 |              "Authorization": f"Bearer {self._bearer()}"},
244 |             self.timeout,
245 |         )
246 | 
247 |     # --- scenario Action step ---
248 |     def draft_action(self, scenario, result) -> str:
249 |         """Draft the Action half of Trigger -> Transmission -> Impact -> Action.
250 | 
251 |         The engine has already computed the impact; Granite argues what the desk
252 |         should do about it, in advance. Numbers are handed in already formatted and
253 |         must be echoed rather than recomputed, so the same provenance rule applies:
254 |         a figure that was not in the brief did not come from the engine.
255 | 
256 |         Returns "" on any failure. The caller renders the computed impact either
257 |         way, so a dead model costs the narrative, never the numbers.
258 |         """
259 |         breaches = result.new_breaches
260 |         breach_line = (
261 |             "None. Every standing belief stays inside its approved range through this shock."
262 |             if not breaches else
263 |             "Breaks: " + ", ".join(f"{b.card_id} ({b.metric})" for b in breaches)
264 |         )
265 |         # Directions, not figures. See ScenarioResult.as_directions for why.
266 |         brief = (
267 |             f"SCENARIO: {scenario.name}\n\n"
268 |             f"TRIGGER\n{scenario.trigger.strip()}\n\n"
269 |             f"TRANSMISSION\n{scenario.transmission.strip()}\n\n"
270 |             f"IMPACT (computed by the desk's engine; you are given directions, not values)\n"
271 |             f"{result.as_directions()}\n\n"
272 |             f"STANDING BELIEFS THIS WOULD BREAK\n  {breach_line}\n\n"
273 |             "Write the ACTION step: what the desk should decide now, before this happens."
274 |         )
275 |         # Two attempts. The watsonx chat endpoint has been seen returning an empty
276 |         # completion for no stated reason, and losing the Action step to one bad
277 |         # round trip during a recording is not worth the saved call.
278 |         last = None
279 |         for attempt in range(2):
280 |             try:
281 |                 return self._draft_once(brief)
282 |             except Exception as e:                              # noqa: BLE001
283 |                 last = e
284 |         print(f"[granite] no action draft: {type(last).__name__}: {last}", file=sys.stderr)
285 |         return ""
286 | 
287 |     def _draft_once(self, brief: str) -> str:
288 |         """One attempt, raising rather than degrading so the retry can see why."""
289 |         raw = self._transport([
290 |             {"role": "system", "content": ACTION_PROMPT},
291 |             {"role": "user", "content": brief},
292 |         ])
293 |         text = raw["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()
294 |         if not text:
295 |             # An empty completion is not a draft. Left unchecked it returns "" and the
296 |             # caller reports the model as unavailable, which is both wrong and silent.
297 |             raise ValueError("model returned an empty action draft")
298 | 
299 |         # The brief carries directions, never measured values, so the model has no
300 |         # figure to echo and nothing to do arithmetic on. The digits it did see are
301 |         # window metadata ("30d", "95"), fine to repeat; anything else it produced
302 |         # itself. Option enumerators are formatting, not claims.
303 |         allowed = _numbers_in(brief)
304 |         invented = [n for n in _NUMBER_RUN.findall(_LIST_MARKER.sub("", text))
305 |                     if n not in allowed]
306 |         if invented:
307 |             raise ValueError(f"model invented {invented} in the action draft")
308 |         return text
309 | 
310 |     # --- the seam ---
311 |     def classify(self, card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach, context: dict) -> Classification:
312 |         try:
313 |             raw = self._transport([
314 |                 {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
315 |                 {"role": "user", "content": _user_prompt(card, breach, context)},
316 |             ])
317 |             return self._parse(raw, card, breach)
318 |         except Exception as e:                                  # noqa: BLE001
319 |             print(f"[granite] falling back to stub: {type(e).__name__}: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
320 |             return self._fallback.classify(card, breach, context)
321 | 
322 |     def _parse(self, raw: dict, card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach) -> Classification:
323 |         content = raw["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
324 |         obj = _extract_json(content)
325 | 
326 |         label = str(obj.get("label", "")).strip().lower()
327 |         if label not in LABELS:
328 |             raise ValueError(f"unknown label from model: {label!r}")
329 | 
330 |         rationale = str(obj.get("rationale", "")).strip()
331 |         if not rationale:
332 |             raise ValueError("model returned an empty rationale")
333 |         invented = _fabricated(rationale, card, breach)
334 |         if invented:
335 |             raise ValueError(f"model invented {invented}, provenance broken: {rationale!r}")
336 | 
337 |         confidence = float(obj.get("confidence", 0.5))
338 |         if not 0.0 <= confidence <= 1.0:
339 |             raise ValueError(f"confidence out of range: {confidence}")
340 | 
341 |         return Classification(label, round(confidence, 2), rationale, source="granite")
342 |

Contents of file orchestrator/classifier.py:

1 | """The seam where IBM Granite plugs in.
2 | 
3 | StubClassifier is a deterministic placeholder so the full Measure -> Challenge ->
4 | Support loop is demoable today. To go live, add a GraniteClassifier with the same
5 | `.classify(card, breach, context)` signature and swap it in — that is the ONLY
6 | change; the pipeline, ledger, and UI are untouched. That is the point of the seam.
7 | 
8 | Granite's real job (NOT what this stub does): read the BeliefCard's claim/rationale
9 | and judge whether the breach changes the underlying *story* — a contract can be over
10 | its numeric band and still be fine if the thesis said so. The stub only compares
11 | numbers; do not mistake it for the semantic step.
12 | """
13 | from __future__ import annotations
14 | 
15 | from dataclasses import dataclass
16 | from typing import Protocol
17 | 
18 | from engine.ledger import BeliefCard, Breach
19 | 
20 | DIRECT_CONTRADICTION = "direct_contradiction"
21 | ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED = "assumption_weakened"
22 | ASSUMPTION_STRENGTHENED = "assumption_strengthened"
23 | NEW_MATERIAL_EVIDENCE = "new_material_evidence"
24 | LABELS = {DIRECT_CONTRADICTION, ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED, ASSUMPTION_STRENGTHENED, NEW_MATERIAL_EVIDENCE}
25 | 
26 | 
27 | @dataclass
28 | class Classification:
29 |     label: str
30 |     confidence: float     # 0-1 stub heuristic; Granite will replace with real judgment
31 |     rationale: str        # placeholder narrative; Granite writes the real one
32 |     source: str = "stub"  # "stub" | "granite"
33 | 
34 | 
35 | class Classifier(Protocol):
36 |     def classify(self, card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach, context: dict) -> Classification: ...
37 | 
38 | 
39 | class StubClassifier:
40 |     """Deterministic heuristic — a placeholder for Granite, NOT semantic reasoning."""
41 | 
42 |     def classify(self, card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach, context: dict) -> Classification:
43 |         band = max(breach.expected_high - breach.expected_low, 1e-9)
44 |         over_by = (breach.observed - breach.expected_high) if breach.direction == "over" \
45 |             else (breach.expected_low - breach.observed)
46 |         frac = over_by / band  # how far past the band, in band-widths
47 | 
48 |         if breach.direction == "over" and "risk" in breach.metric:
49 |             label = DIRECT_CONTRADICTION if frac > 0.5 else ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED
50 |             rationale = (
51 |                 f"Observed {breach.metric} = {breach.observed:.1f} exceeds the belief's ceiling "
52 |                 f"of {breach.expected_high:.1f}; the position is taking more risk than the thesis "
53 |                 f"called deliberate."
54 |             )
55 |         elif breach.direction == "under" and "return" in breach.metric:
56 |             label = ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED
57 |             rationale = (
58 |                 f"{breach.metric} = {breach.observed:.1f} fell below the floor {breach.expected_low:.1f}; "
59 |                 f"the position is not being paid for the risk it consumes — the 'compensated' premise "
60 |                 f"is eroding."
61 |             )
62 |         else:
63 |             label = ASSUMPTION_WEAKENED
64 |             rationale = (
65 |                 f"{breach.metric} = {breach.observed:.1f} is outside "
66 |                 f"[{breach.expected_low:.1f}, {breach.expected_high:.1f}]."
67 |             )
68 |         return Classification(label, round(min(0.5 + frac, 0.95), 2), rationale, source="stub")

Contents of file engine/gap.py:

1 | """The catalyst contract: can this company fund itself to its own next readout?
2 | 
3 |     funding gap = runway exhaustion date - registered primary completion date
4 | 
5 | Negative means the money runs out first. Both sides are deterministic and sourced:
6 | the left from SEC XBRL tags, the right from the sponsor's own registry filing. No
7 | model contributes a number to either.
8 | 
9 | What makes this more than a subtraction is the third column. The registry date is not
10 | an observation, it is a *claim the sponsor can revise at will*, and every revision is
11 | timestamped. So alongside the gap we carry the sponsor's revision behaviour: how many
12 | times the date moved, and how much notice was given each time. A company that pushes a
13 | readout eighteen months out with a year of warning is forecasting. One that moves it
14 | three weeks before it arrives was showing a date it had stopped believing -- and if
15 | that behaviour turns out to cluster in cash-constrained sponsors, the registry is not
16 | a data source, it is a disclosure channel with incentives.
17 | 
18 | That last claim is a hypothesis, not a result. This module assembles the panel needed
19 | to test it. It does not test it, and nothing here should be presented as if it had.
20 | """
21 | from __future__ import annotations
22 | 
23 | import json
24 | import urllib.parse
25 | from dataclasses import dataclass
26 | from datetime import date
27 | 
28 | from engine.ctgov_history import TrialHistory, _get, _parse_date, fetch_history
29 | from engine.runway import Runway, compute_runway, ticker_to_cik
30 | 
31 | V2 = "https://clinicaltrials.gov/api/v2/studies"
32 | # Phase 2/3 only. Early-phase trials are not value-inflection points, and including
33 | # them buries the catalyst in a pile of dose-escalation studies.
34 | PIVOTAL_PHASES = {"PHASE2", "PHASE3", "PHASE2_PHASE3"}
35 | LIVE = {"RECRUITING", "ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING", "ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION", "NOT_YET_RECRUITING"}
36 | 
37 | 
38 | def find_trials(sponsor: str, limit: int = 40) -> list[dict]:
39 |     """Live pivotal-ish trials for one lead sponsor.
40 | 
41 |     The sponsor field is free text with no CIK, so this join is the engineering tax
42 |     of the whole project: matching is by name, and names drift (subsidiaries, "Inc."
43 |     vs "Inc", post-merger renames). Good enough to demo one company; a universe run
44 |     needs an alias table and hand review of the largest names.
45 |     """
46 |     q = urllib.parse.urlencode({
47 |         "query.spons": sponsor,
48 |         "fields": ",".join([
49 |             "protocolSection.identificationModule.nctId",
50 |             "protocolSection.identificationModule.briefTitle",
51 |             "protocolSection.statusModule.overallStatus",
52 |             "protocolSection.statusModule.primaryCompletionDateStruct",
53 |             "protocolSection.designModule.phases",
54 |             "protocolSection.sponsorCollaboratorsModule.leadSponsor",
55 |         ]),
56 |         "pageSize": limit,
57 |     })
58 |     out = []
59 |     for s in _get(f"{V2}?{q}").get("studies", []):
60 |         p = s.get("protocolSection", {})
61 |         phases = set(p.get("designModule", {}).get("phases", []) or [])
62 |         status = p.get("statusModule", {}).get("overallStatus", "")
63 |         pcd = p.get("statusModule", {}).get("primaryCompletionDateStruct", {}) or {}
64 |         if not (phases & PIVOTAL_PHASES) or status not in LIVE or not pcd.get("date"):
65 |             continue
66 |         out.append({
67 |             "nct": p["identificationModule"]["nctId"],
68 |             "title": p["identificationModule"].get("briefTitle", ""),
69 |             "status": status,
70 |             "pcd": pcd["date"],
71 |             "pcd_type": pcd.get("type", ""),
72 |             "phases": sorted(phases),
73 |             "sponsor": p.get("sponsorCollaboratorsModule", {}).get("leadSponsor", {}).get("name", ""),
74 |         })
75 |     return sorted(out, key=lambda t: t["pcd"])
76 | 
77 | 
78 | @dataclass
79 | class CatalystContract:
80 |     """One testable claim: this company reaches this readout on this money."""
81 |     runway: Runway
82 |     trial: dict
83 |     history: TrialHistory | None = None
84 | 
85 |     @property
86 |     def catalyst_date(self) -> date | None:
87 |         return _parse_date(self.trial["pcd"])
88 | 
89 |     @property
90 |     def gap_months(self) -> float | None:
91 |         """Months of runway left at the registered readout date. Negative = short.
92 | 
93 |         Computed against the CONSERVATIVE end of the burn band. Reporting the
94 |         optimistic end would flatter every row in the same direction.
95 |         """
96 |         end = self.runway.exhaustion(self.runway.months_low)
97 |         cat = self.catalyst_date
98 |         if end is None or cat is None:
99 |             return None
100 |         return (end - cat).days / (365.25 / 12)
101 | 
102 |     @property
103 |     def verdict(self) -> str:
104 |         g = self.gap_months
105 |         if g is None:
106 |             return "not computable"
107 |         if not self.runway.reliable:
108 |             return "not rankable (burn estimate unreliable)"
109 |         return "funded to catalyst" if g >= 0 else "financing required before catalyst"
110 | 
111 |     def lines(self) -> list[str]:
112 |         r, t = self.runway, self.trial
113 |         g = self.gap_months
114 |         out = [
115 |             f"{r.ticker}  {r.name}",
116 |             f"  liquidity      ${r.liquidity/1e6:,.0f}M  ({r.provenance['cash']} + {r.provenance['securities']}, as of {r.as_of})",
117 |             f"  burn band      ${r.burn_ttm_annual/1e6:,.0f}M/yr trailing, ${r.burn_recent_annual/1e6:,.0f}M/yr most recent quarter",
118 |             f"  runway         {r.months_low:.1f}-{r.months_high:.1f} months, exhausted {r.exhaustion(r.months_low)}",
119 |             f"  catalyst       {t['nct']}  {'/'.join(t['phases'])}  {t['status']}",
120 |             f"                 {t['title'][:70]}",
121 |             f"                 primary completion {t['pcd']} ({t['pcd_type'].lower()})",
122 |             f"  FUNDING GAP    {'n/a' if g is None else f'{g:+.1f} months'}   -> {self.verdict}",
123 |         ]
124 |         if self.history and self.history.revisions:
125 |             h = self.history
126 |             out += [
127 |                 f"  date integrity {len(h.revisions)} revisions across {h.n_versions} versions, "
128 |                 f"net slip {h.total_slip_days:+d} days, {h.n_late_moves} moved with under 90 days notice",
129 |             ]
130 |             if h.n_expired_carried:
131 |                 out.append(f"  *** EXPIRED     showed an already-passed completion date for up to "
132 |                            f"{h.max_days_expired} days before correcting it")
133 |             for rev in h.revisions[-4:]:
134 |                 if rev.carried_expired:
135 |                     notice = f"{rev.days_expired:>4}d EXPIRED"
136 |                 elif rev.held_days is None:
137 |                     notice = ""
138 |                 else:
139 |                     notice = f"{rev.held_days:>5}d notice"
140 |                 moved = "" if rev.moved_days is None else f"{rev.moved_days:+5d}d"
141 |                 out.append(f"                 v{rev.version:<4} {rev.submitted}  -> {rev.pcd}  {moved}  {notice}")
142 |         for n in r.notes:
143 |             out.append(f"  note           {n}")
144 |         return out

147 | def build(ticker: str, cik_map=None, with_history: bool = True) -> CatalystContract | None:
148 |     r = compute_runway(ticker, cik_map)
149 |     trials = find_trials(r.name)
150 |     if not trials:
151 |         return None
152 |     # The nearest live pivotal readout is the binding catalyst.
153 |     trial = trials[0]
154 |     hist = None
155 |     if with_history:
156 |         try:
157 |             hist = fetch_history(trial["nct"])
158 |         except (RuntimeError, ValueError):
159 |             pass  # history is an enrichment, not a precondition
160 |     return CatalystContract(runway=r, trial=trial, history=hist)

176 |             recomputed = (c.runway.exhaustion(c.runway.months_low) - c.catalyst_date).days
177 |             assert abs(recomputed / (365.25 / 12) - c.gap_months) < 1e-9

Contents of file orchestrator/challenge.py:

1 | """Challenge pipeline: turn a breach into a bounded, human-answerable review, then
2 | apply the human's decision to the governed stores.
3 | 
4 | Flow:  breach -> build_challenge (classify + redline + memo)  ->  human Decision
5 |        -> apply_decision  ->  approve/edit: ledger.update  |  reject: review-log
6 | 
7 | Three separate stores (as designed): the BeliefLedger (approvals only), the
8 | ReviewLog (rejections + why), and the eval-set (later — reviewed examples become
9 | regression tests). Nothing here computes a risk number, and nothing changes a
10 | belief without an explicit human verdict.
11 | """
12 | from __future__ import annotations
13 | 
14 | from dataclasses import dataclass, field, replace
15 | import json
16 | import os
17 | import time
18 | 
19 | from engine.ledger import BeliefCard, BeliefLedger, Breach
20 | from orchestrator.classifier import Classifier, StubClassifier, Classification
21 | 
22 | ACCEPT, REDUCE, HEDGE = "accept", "reduce", "hedge"
23 | 
24 | # Structured fields the human answers on the challenge card (bounded, not free chat).
25 | QUESTIONS = [
26 |     {"field": "intentional", "prompt": "Is this exposure intentional or accidental?",
27 |      "options": ["intentional", "accidental"]},
28 |     {"field": "conviction", "prompt": "Conviction in the driver now (1-5)?",
29 |      "options": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]},
30 |     {"field": "thesis", "prompt": "One-line thesis for the disposition.", "options": None},
31 |     {"field": "invalidation", "prompt": "What would prove this wrong?", "options": None},
32 |     {"field": "action", "prompt": "Action?", "options": [ACCEPT, REDUCE, HEDGE]},
33 | ]
34 | 
35 | 
36 | @dataclass
37 | class ChallengeCard:
38 |     card_id: str
39 |     breach: Breach
40 |     classification: Classification
41 |     memo: str                    # narrative draft (stub; Granite replaces)
42 |     proposed_card: BeliefCard    # the "accept new reality" redline vs the current card
43 |     questions: list = field(default_factory=lambda: QUESTIONS)
44 | 
45 |     def redline(self) -> str:
46 |         """Human-readable diff of the proposed change vs the current belief."""
47 |         cur, new = self.breach, self.proposed_card
48 |         if new.expected_low != cur.expected_low:
49 |             return f"expected_low: {cur.expected_low:.1f} -> {new.expected_low:.1f}"
50 |         if new.expected_high != cur.expected_high:
51 |             return f"expected_high: {cur.expected_high:.1f} -> {new.expected_high:.1f}"
52 |         return "no range change proposed"
53 | 
54 | 
55 | def build_challenge(card: BeliefCard, breach: Breach, context: dict | None = None,
56 |                     classifier: Classifier | None = None) -> ChallengeCard:
57 |     classifier = classifier or StubClassifier()
58 |     cls = classifier.classify(card, breach, context or {})
59 | 
60 |     # Proposed redline = "accept the new reality": widen the band to include the
61 |     # observed value. Approving this says "yes, this exposure is acceptable now."
62 |     if breach.direction == "over":
63 |         proposed = replace(card, expected_high=round(breach.observed, 1))
64 |     else:
65 |         proposed = replace(card, expected_low=round(breach.observed, 1))
66 | 
67 |     memo = (
68 |         f"CHALLENGE [{cls.label}]  {card.card_id} (v{card.version})  conf {cls.confidence}\n"
69 |         f"  Belief : {card.claim}\n"
70 |         f"  Driver : {card.driver} (conviction {card.confidence}/5)\n"
71 |         f"  Reading: {breach.metric} = {breach.observed:.1f}, expected "
72 |         f"[{breach.expected_low:.1f}, {breach.expected_high:.1f}]\n"
73 |         f"  {cls.rationale}\n"
74 |         # Every number above is rendered here from the engine's output; the classifier
75 |         # supplies only the judgment sentence. The tag keeps that provenance visible.
76 |         + ("  [judgment drafted by IBM Granite]" if cls.source == "granite" else
77 |            "  [stub judgment — IBM Granite drafts this from the RiskPacket + news context]")
78 |     )
79 |     return ChallengeCard(card.card_id, breach, cls, memo, proposed)
80 | 
81 | 
82 | @dataclass
83 | class Decision:
84 |     verdict: str                      # "approve" | "edit" | "reject"
85 |     author: str                       # e.g. "human:kristen" — this is the governance gate
86 |     reason: str = ""
87 |     edited_card: BeliefCard | None = None   # required for "edit"
88 |     answers: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
89 | 
90 | 
91 | class ReviewLog:
92 |     """3rd store: why a challenge was rejected. Append-only, never touches the ledger."""
93 | 
94 |     def __init__(self, path: str):
95 |         self.path = path
96 | 
97 |     def append(self, challenge: ChallengeCard, decision: Decision) -> dict:
98 |         os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(self.path) or ".", exist_ok=True)
99 |         rec = {
100 |             "ts": time.time(),
101 |             "card_id": challenge.card_id,
102 |             "classification": challenge.classification.label,
103 |             "verdict": decision.verdict,
104 |             "reason": decision.reason,
105 |             "author": decision.author,
106 |             "observed": challenge.breach.observed,
107 |             "answers": decision.answers,
108 |         }
109 |         with open(self.path, "a") as f:
110 |             f.write(json.dumps(rec) + "\n")
111 |         return rec
112 | 
113 |     def all(self) -> list[dict]:
114 |         if not os.path.exists(self.path):
115 |             return []
116 |         with open(self.path) as f:
117 |             return [json.loads(line) for line in f if line.strip()]
118 | 
119 | 
120 | def apply_decision(ledger: BeliefLedger, review_log: ReviewLog,
121 |                    challenge: ChallengeCard, decision: Decision):
122 |     """Route the human verdict to the right store. Approve/edit -> ledger version
123 |     bump; reject -> review log. The ledger is the ONLY thing an approval mutates."""
124 |     if decision.verdict in ("approve", "edit"):
125 |         card = decision.edited_card if decision.verdict == "edit" else challenge.proposed_card
126 |         if card is None:
127 |             raise ValueError("edit verdict requires edited_card")
128 |         return ledger.update(
129 |             card, author=decision.author,
130 |             triggered_by=f"breach:{challenge.card_id}",
131 |             reason=decision.reason or challenge.classification.label,
132 |         )
133 |     if decision.verdict == "reject":
134 |         return review_log.append(challenge, decision)   # belief stands; ledger untouched
135 |     raise ValueError(f"unknown verdict: {decision.verdict}")
136 |
